title: Sleeping Gods: Primeval Peril (Review)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hD4BjVn9YXw

Daniel (@dungeondive) explores Sleeping Gods: Primeval Peril, an expertly distilled compact version of the beloved expansive adventure series. At under $40 and roughly the size of Warhammer Quest or Elder Sign, this is Sleeping Gods for players who want the epic narrative and survival tension without the furniture-moving commitment. The setting is a single river system, which elegantly constrains the scope—you know you're following a narrative path with branches, not swimming in an infinite sandbox.

The mechanical compression is masterful: combat encounters live on the map pages rather than in separate card decks, the 69-card adventure deck handles quests and items alike, and the crew size shrinks to five characters who genuinely feel like a unified team rather than five separate stat blocks to juggle. You're still managing health, stamina, and event cards (which conspire against you constantly), collecting blood flowers to level up your team, and hitting a hard time limit before the abducted crew member is lost forever. The resource tension is exquisite—health is currency here, rest is precious, and tough decisions are constant. Daniel especially loves that at $37-40, this represents genuine value alongside the elegant design.

----

For Sleeping Gods fans, does the compact format appeal to you, or is bigger always better? What's your favorite narrative-driven adventure game?
